01-25.title: Slammer worm anniversary.
01-25.note: In 2003, the SQL Slammer worm infected 75,000 hosts in 10 minutes. Patches existed six months before. Patch velocity still defines survivability.

02-07.title: Safer Internet Day.
02-07.note: A good reminder to review MFA enrollment across your identity provider. Pockets of SMS-based fallback are still the #1 account takeover vector I see.

03-01.title: Start of fiscal Q-planning.
03-01.note: If you haven't mapped this year's security controls to MITRE ATT&CK and your actual incidents, now is the week.

03-31.title: World Backup Day.
03-31.note: Restore-tested backups are the only backups. Pull a random workload from cold storage today and time the restore.

04-07.title: Heartbleed disclosed (2014).
04-07.note: The bug that normalized 'patch the entire internet.' A decent prompt to audit what TLS libraries your vendors statically link.

05-12.title: WannaCry anniversary (2017).
05-12.note: A kill switch saved the NHS. Your SMB exposure audit should run at least quarterly.

06-27.title: NotPetya anniversary (2017).
06-27.note: $10B in damage from a supply-chain wiper. Revisit your software build-provenance today.

07-04.title: Holiday attack surface.
07-04.note: Long weekends are ransomware prime time. Cross-check your on-call rotation and EDR alert routing.

08-01.title: DEF CON season.
08-01.note: Expect fresh research drops. Subscribe to the PacketStorm advisory RSS if you haven't already.

09-18.title: Equifax breach disclosed (2017).
09-18.note: An unpatched Struts vulnerability exposed 147M records. The post-mortem is still required reading for any patch program.

10-01.title: Cybersecurity Awareness Month.
10-01.note: Skip the posters. Run a phishing simulation tied to a real breach story and post the results publicly.

11-02.title: Morris worm (1988).
11-02.note: The first internet-scale worm, by a PhD student, from an MIT host. Every modern vuln chain descends from this template.

12-10.title: Log4Shell anniversary (2021).
12-10.note: A reminder to audit your SBOMs. Six-figure vendor products still ship with vulnerable log4j 2.x under a dozen rebrands.

12-13.title: SolarWinds disclosed (2020).
12-13.note: Supply-chain compromise via a signed update. Your vendor inventory should include which products auto-update without review.
